title: London in 48 Hours
intro: London does not have a centre so much as a current — the Thames — and the smart weekend moves with it. Day 1 rides the classic sweep from Westminster's bells along the South Bank to Borough Market's banter, up a free garden in the sky and into a Covent Garden dining room older than the United States. Day 2 belongs to the other London: Sunday flower markets shouted in Cockney, a salt beef beigel on Brick Lane, a riverboat down to the Prime Meridian, and a last pint under bronze monks. Two days, ten stops, and a thousand years of city underfoot.

days.label: Day 1
days.subtitle: Westminster, the South Bank & a Covent Garden Evening

blocks.title: Bells, Pelicans and Empty Pavements
blocks.body: Start before the coach tours do, when Westminster Bridge belongs to joggers and the freshly regilded Elizabeth Tower catches the low light over a river with almost no one on it. This is the most photographed view in Britain and it genuinely is better at half past eight. Loop through St James's Park on the way — the lake's resident pelicans, descendants of a Russian ambassador's 1664 gift, patrol the willows with Buckingham Palace shimmering at the far end of the water — then come back along Whitehall past Horse Guards for your dose of royal pageantry without a single ticket.
blocks.tip: Sightseeing entry to Westminster Abbey runs about £30 — attending Choral Evensong instead (usually 5 p.m. weekdays, 3 p.m. weekends) is free and arguably the better experience.

blocks.title: A Power Station Full of Rothkos
blocks.body: Walk east along the South Bank — past the London Eye, the brutalist terraces of the Southbank Centre and the secondhand book stalls under Waterloo Bridge — until the brick chimney of a decommissioned power station rises over Bankside. Inside, the Turbine Hall's cathedral-scale commissions set the tone and the free collection runs from Picasso through Rothko to Kusama. Don't leave without riding up to the viewing terraces, where St Paul's dome sits perfectly framed across the Millennium Bridge like the city planned it that way. It did not, which is somehow more satisfying.
blocks.tip: The collection is free with no ticket needed — save your money for the blockbuster exhibitions, and take the riverside walk slowly; the detours are the point.

blocks.title: Lunch Under the Green Ironwork
blocks.body: A market has traded on this spot for a thousand years, and Saturday is Borough at full roar — oyster shuckers, cheesemongers, and the smell of Kappacasein's raclette being scraped molten over potatoes under the Victorian ironwork. Graze rather than commit: a Bread Ahead doughnut here, Neal's Yard cheese there, a flat white from the Monmouth queue that looks alarming and moves in minutes. If you want to sit down, Padella's fresh pasta at the market's edge — the pici cacio e pepe is the order — costs trattoria prices for some of the best plates in Britain.
blocks.tip: Padella takes no reservations — join the virtual queue on the app the moment you arrive at the market, then graze the stalls until your table pings.

blocks.title: A Free Garden in the Sky
blocks.body: Cross London Bridge into the Square Mile and detour through Leadenhall Market, the maroon-and-gold Victorian arcade that moonlighted as Diagon Alley, on your way to the 'Walkie-Talkie' tower at 20 Fenchurch Street. Thirty-five floors up, three storeys of ferns and figs curl beneath a curved glass canopy in London's highest public garden — and the 360-degree panorama, with the Shard, Tower Bridge and the Tower of London lined up below the south terrace, is completely free. It goes toe-to-toe with the paid decks across the river, which is why the booking page is a bloodsport.
blocks.tip: Free timed tickets release online about a week ahead and weekend slots vanish within hours — book the moment your dates are fixed and bring photo ID. Sold out? A drink reservation at the Sky Pod bar gets you up regardless, and Horizon 22 nearby is the free backup.

blocks.title: Dinner at London's Oldest Table, Then a Hanky Panky
blocks.body: London's oldest restaurant has been feeding the city since 1798, and the room shows every glorious year of it — velvet banquettes, taxidermy from the house's own Teesdale estate, and walls crowded with two centuries of theatrical gossip. Dickens dined here and Graham Greene kept his own table. Order the steak and kidney pudding or whatever the kitchen is doing with venison, finish with spotted dick and proper custard, and embrace the ritual. Then walk three minutes to the Savoy, where the American Bar has been mixing drinks since the 1890s: the Hanky Panky was invented on this spot by Ada Coleman, and drinking one where it was born is a London rite of passage.
blocks.tip: Book Rules one to two weeks ahead and ask for a booth; the upstairs cocktail bar is a hidden gem for a pre-dinner martini if you arrive early.

days.label: Day 2
days.subtitle: East End Markets, Maritime Greenwich & a Soho Nightcap

blocks.title: Two Bunches a Fiver
blocks.body: Every Sunday morning this narrow East End street disappears under banana palms, peonies and trays of seedlings, and the barrow boys holler 'two bunches a fiver!' in unreconstructed Cockney over the heads of the crowd. Come at half past eight and you can still move; the Victorian shopfronts lining the road — tiny galleries, vintage shops, cafés doing a brisk trade in bacon rolls — open to match the market. Buy a bunch of something you can't take home just to be part of the transaction. This is London's best free theatre, and it smells wonderful.
blocks.tip: The market is Sundays only, roughly 8 a.m. to 3 p.m. — cash speeds up the barrow transactions, and prices drop sharply in the final hour if you'd rather come for bargains than calm.

blocks.title: The Salt Beef Beigel Question
blocks.body: Ten minutes' walk south, Brick Lane tells London's immigration story in a single street — a Huguenot chapel that became a synagogue and is now the Jamme Masjid mosque — and serves its definitive cheap breakfast. At Beigel Bake, the white-fronted bakery that never closes, hand-carved salt beef goes into a chewy, still-warm beigel with ferocious mustard and a pickle for about the price of a Mayfair espresso. The yellow-fronted Beigel Shop next door has its own fierce partisans; picking a side is a local tradition. Eat it walking past the street art and the Sunday market stalls spilling out of the Truman Brewery.
blocks.tip: The queue moves fast and the debate is eternal — if you can't choose, get one from each and conduct your own referendum on the walk south past Old Spitalfields Market.

blocks.title: Down the River to the Meridian
blocks.body: Walk down past the Tower of London to the pier and board an Uber Boat: the Thames Clipper catamarans are commuter transport, which makes them the best-value cruise in the city, and Greenwich was always meant to be approached by water. Tower Bridge slides behind you, Wapping's warehouse canyons give way to Canary Wharf's glass, and then the baroque waterfront of Wren's Old Royal Naval College appears like a stage set. Ashore, graze the covered stalls of Greenwich Market, straddle the Prime Meridian at the Royal Observatory with one foot in each hemisphere, and circle the Cutty Sark, the world's last tea clipper, raised gleaming in her glass dry dock. The view from the Observatory hill across the park to the towers downstream is one of London's finest.
blocks.tip: Tap in with contactless like the Tube and sit outside at the stern for photos; the Observatory's last entry is around 4:15 p.m., so do the hill before the market if you're running behind.

blocks.title: Bronze Monks and a Proper Pint
blocks.body: Ride the boat back upriver as the light goes golden and hop off at Blackfriars Pier, where the most magnificently bonkers pub in London stands in its wedge of land like a ship's prow. Built on the site of a Dominican friary and remodelled in 1905 into an Arts and Crafts fantasia, The Blackfriar drips with bronze monks, golden mosaics and marble inscriptions — 'Wisdom is Rare', 'Finery is Foolery' — that will judge you gently as you drink. Order a pint of Fuller's London Pride, claim a spot in the Grotto at the back, and toast John Betjeman, whose campaign saved the building from the wrecking ball in the 1960s.
blocks.tip: The back room — the Grotto — has the most extravagant decoration; Sunday evenings are mellow, and the riverside walk from the pier takes about two minutes.

blocks.title: Hoppers, Sambols and the Soho Night Shift
blocks.body: End the weekend on Frith Street, where Hoppers packed Sri Lankan and Tamil home cooking into a tiny room and started a citywide obsession. The namesake hopper — a bowl-shaped fermented rice pancake with a soft egg baked into its centre — arrives with fiery sambols and coconut relishes; add the bone marrow varuval with buttery roti, the sleeper hit everyone orders on their second visit. Afterwards you're spoiled within fifty metres: Ronnie Scott's has been swinging in its basement at number 47 since 1959, and Bar Italia at number 22 has poured espresso to cabbies, musicians and insomniacs beneath its Rocky Marciano shrine since 1949. A double at the chrome counter, a pavement seat, and Soho's night shift flowing past — that's the correct last scene.
blocks.tip: The Soho original takes no bookings — walk in before 6 p.m. or after 9 p.m., or put your name down and have a drink up the street while you wait.
